issues.title: Error: "Attempted to send a key down event when no keys are in keysPressed" on Pressing Select or Enter on a TV remote
issues.body:
I'm using **onKey** and **onKeyEvent** to listen to tv remote control keys
but I have this error when press **SELECT** or **ENTER**

```
Attempted to send a key down event when no keys are in keysPressed. This state can occur if the key event being sent doesn't properly set its modifier flags. This was the event: RawKeyDownEvent#23bc9(logicalKey: LogicalKeyboardKey#0050c(keyId: "0x10000050c", keyLabel: "Select", debugName: "Select"), physicalKey: PhysicalKeyboardKey#700e4(usbHidUsage: "0x000700e4", debugName: "Control Right"), repeat: false) and its data: RawKeyEventDataAndroid#92581(flags: 8, codePoint: 0, plainCodePoint: 0, keyCode: 23, scanCode: 97, metaState: 0)
'package:flutter/src/services/raw_keyboard.dart':
package:flutter/…/services/raw_keyboard.dart:1
Failed assertion: line 685 pos 7: 'event is! RawKeyDownEvent || _keysPressed.isNotEmpty'`
```
When I use **RawKeyboardListener** I have same issue but it call the method **onKey**

issues.title: [iOS] - Dictation deltas don't respect app-side content updates
issues.body:
On iOS, when using dictation, the IME deltas that are dispatched to apps are delivered in a way that causes the app to get out of sync with the dictation system.

I've described the issue as it presents in Super Editor: https://github.com/superlistapp/super_editor/issues/1005

The summary of the issue is: The dictation system sends us one delta at a time, but the dictation system has already assembled multiple deltas that depend on each other. In cases where multiple deltas relate to each other, we're supposed to get the whole set of deltas at once. Instead, we get the deltas one at a time, which is supposed to let us immediately send our own app-side updates to the IME to alter the content (if we choose to) before the next delta comes in. But the next delta that comes in is based on the content of the first delta, which is now out of date.

Flutter version:
```
Flutter 3.8.0-12.0.pre.57 • channel master • https://github.com/flutter/flutter.git
Framework • revision 480c54c370 (82 minutes ago) • 2023-02-14 13:18:12 -0800
Engine • revision f4fcb911be
Tools • Dart 3.0.0 (build 3.0.0-233.0.dev) • DevTools 2.21.1
```
comments:
@justinmc @Renzo-Olivares - Can you please check the IME implementation for iOS and see whether this issue sits inside the dictation system, or whether it's how Flutter chooses to batch and submit deltas?

I'm hoping it's something that Flutter is doing, and that you can quickly and easily batch the deltas the way they should be.

I tried to verify and possibly replicate this reported behavior based on the details provided in [this](https://github.com/superlistapp/super_editor/issues/1005) issue and running the super_editor on iOS physical device but was unable to replicate it. I tried below scenarios: 1. Use microphone and speak that will go to next line. 2. Speak again that will go to next line. 3. Stop microphone which didn't remove the text entered in step 2 above. 4. Also tried to type-in using keyboard that will go to next line. 5. Use microphone which didn't remove the text entered in step 4. I also tried using below simple `TextField` with multi-line enabled and repeated above steps which didn't replicate the behavior. I am not sure if above scenarios would properly help to replicate the reported behavior in attached issue, but based on the report, I am going ahead and labeling it for team's attention and insights.
Can you also try contractions on a line after the first line? For example: I'm, you're, we're, they're, etc.

On Super Editor we're now getting reports of many Samsung device errors and I'm concerned that it might be this same problem: https://github.com/superlistapp/super_editor/issues/1039

@justinmc can you have someone try to root cause this? If the Samsung problem is the same issue, then this is now a serious issue for Super Editor. Our clients are getting a lot of crash reports because the IME deltas seem to be out of sync with our app-side representation.
Hi @matthew-carroll i'll take a look at this and see if this issue is on the framework side or the iOS TextInputPlugin.
@Renzo-Olivares Any progress on this? We're still getting reports that dictated text is immediately deleted on iOS with super_editor.

I've been looking into this but haven't landed at a solution yet. Here are some observations from my investigation.

The dictation system on iOS is actively replacing the entire dictation range with the new value that it determines is the correct dictation.

Saying `Hello`, the dictation system will:

insert 'H'
replace 'H' with 'He'
replace 'He' with 'Hel'
replace 'Hel' with 'Hell'
replace 'Hell' with 'Hello'

This does not always happen character by character, as the dictation system might first detect 'He' and insert that first.

The dictation system may also actively replace words that it has previously inserted if it determines a better dictation based on new words you have spoken.

For example:

`This on is`

and then saying `difficult`, may cause the dictated text to change to `This one is difficult`. In this case `This on is` is replaced with `This one is difficult`.

On the Flutter side we see the entire replacement of the dictation value and try to break it up as best as possible into the determined `TextEditingDelta`.

Going back to the first example

Saying `Hello`:

iOS Dictation|Flutter
---|---
insert 'H'|TextEditingDeltaInsertion('H',0)
replace 'H' with 'He'|TextEditingDeltaInsertion('e',1)
replace 'He' with 'Hel'|TextEditingDeltaInsertion('l',2)
replace 'Hel' with 'Hell'|TextEditingDeltaInsertion('l',3)
replace 'Hell' with 'Hello'|TextEditingDeltaInsertion('o',4)

And the second example

`This on is` -> `This one is difficult`
iOS Dictation|Flutter
---|---
replace 'This on is' with 'This one is difficult'|TextEditingDeltaReplacement(' is', 'e is difficult')

Finally when pressing the microphone to turn dictation off, the dictation system will remove the entire dictation and then insert a final dictation that it may have determined to be a better fit then the already entered dictation. This may be the same or different than the original text.

From what I understand the problem you are having is that the dictation is sending you a new delta (based on the previous delta) before you can send it your modified value, and that is causing `super_editor` to get out of sync. I think this is a case of the edits on the engine happening too fast so they diverge from the framework state.

What you want is that when dictating either the engine waits until the dictation has finished to send the deltas, or you want the dictation to wait for you to confirm before sending more deltas as described here https://github.com/superlistapp/super_editor/issues/1005#issuecomment-1430474763. I think the former might result in a poor UX because it would most likely need to wait until the microphone button is pressed again to stop dictation and send the accumulated deltas, resulting in no text input until dictation is finished instead of an expected live feed. I'm unsure how possible the latter solution is but I can explore it.

@Renzo-Olivares I think a big part of this question is what the Flutter contract is supposed to be. 

Putting aside Super Editor and dictation for a moment, it has historically always been the case with deltas that when a list of deltas arrive, those deltas are a batch, or transaction, and they are referentially separate from deltas that follow.

Perhaps this was incidental, rather than intentional. You'll have to tell me. But that was the case, and that was an important detail in the ability to implement the application of deltas to a structure that's more complex than a `TextEditingValue`.

Therefore, first, Flutter should decide, based on clear evidence from how multiple platforms send events, whether the concept of a transaction can be upheld, or not. What you're describing about iOS dictation sounds like it probably can't work in a world of delta transactions. But you should make absolutely sure of that before losing the power of transactions. You should also check Android dictation and see if you get any different results.

If delta transactions cannot be enforced, then it's important that Flutter demonstrate how deltas can be processed and applied for data structures other than `TextEditingValue`s. If there are no transactions, then when, exactly, is an app permitted to inject its own adjustment to content? We know we can't inject our own changed between deltas in a single list of deltas, but now we can't inject changes between notifications of deltas? This seems to indicate that there's no dependable time or place for an app to alter the IME content. Clearly, that's a problem.

> On the Flutter side we see the entire replacement of the dictation value and try to break it up as best as possible

Why are you altering what the platform IME is sending to Flutter? If the real IME is asking for a full replacement, then why not forward that replacement message to the app? I'm not sure why Flutter should have any influence on what's reported.

> Finally when pressing the microphone to turn dictation off

Does iOS notify the client when dictation starts and stops? If so, is Flutter forwarding this information? If not, Flutter certainly should. Because if this problem is unique to dictation, then we may be able to create an alternate code path to deal with this specifically.

> Putting aside Super Editor and dictation for a moment, it has historically always been the case with deltas that when a list of deltas arrive, those deltas are a batch, or transaction, and they are referentially separate from deltas that follow.

I'm not sure I completely understand what you mean by this. Do you mean that given delta batches A, B, C, that C should be able to be applied without first applying A?

> If delta transactions cannot be enforced, then it's important that Flutter demonstrate how deltas can be processed and applied for data structures other than TextEditingValues. If there are no transactions, then when, exactly, is an app permitted to inject its own adjustment to content? We know we can't inject our own changed between deltas in a single list of deltas, but now we can't inject changes between notifications of deltas? This seems to indicate that there's no dependable time or place for an app to alter the IME content. Clearly, that's a problem.

I agree that its not clear when a TextInputClient implementer can inject their own changes into the editing state, and I need to give it some more thought before giving a concrete answer.

> Why are you altering what the platform IME is sending to Flutter? If the real IME is asking for a full replacement, then why not forward that replacement message to the app? I'm not sure why Flutter should have any influence on what's reported.

iirc the decision for this was because on Android it was observed that the composing region was always active resulting in constant `replacements` instead of an `insertion` or `deletion`. I think some context for this decision can be found here https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/28175#discussion_r698937876 . I don't believe that this abstraction of information is causing any issue with regards to this problem, is there cases where you think this might cause an issue?

> Does iOS notify the client when dictation starts and stops? If so, is Flutter forwarding this information? If not, Flutter certainly should. Because if this problem is unique to dictation, then we may be able to create an alternate code path to deal with this specifically.

It looks like apple provides an API to determine when the dictation ends https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uitextinput/1614507-dictationrecordingdidend, but i'm not seeing the same for when a dictation starts. I think we might be able to leverage [`currentInputModeDidChangeNotification`](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uitextinputmode/1614517-currentinputmodedidchangenotific) and [`activeInputModes`](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uitextinputmode/1614522-activeinputmodes) to determine this, but I will investigate that further and see if its feasible.

> I'm not sure I completely understand what you mean by this. Do you mean that given delta batches A, B, C, that C should be able to be applied without first applying A?

No. What I mean is that an editor should be able to apply "C" without any knowledge or memory that at some point in the past, "B" was applied. This dictation issue shows us that this currently is not the case. When "C" comes in, it's based on the result of "B", even though we've tried to change things after "B" and before "C". This is the transaction argument that I'm making.

> I don't believe that this abstraction of information is causing any issue with regards to this problem, is there cases where you think this might cause an issue?

Given that this issue is still being investigated, and that there's no clear proposal for a solution, I think it's far too early to draw conclusions about the source of the problem. There's this stream of events that's arriving at unknown times, with unknown historical assumptions, and Flutter is applying a greedy algorithm that transforms that information before it gets to the app developer. That transformation could easily prove to be a problem. But it seems like there hasn't been much of an investigation into the nuances of this IME communication, so we don't know. 

What we **do know** for sure, is that other apps have built editors on top of the iOS and Android IME messages. Therefore, the exact messages that come from the platform IME can be used, in some fashion, to implement this desired behavior. What we **don't know** is that Flutter's transformed stream of messages can be used to do that, because no one has done it yet. There are only a handful of Flutter apps and packages that have implemented partial functionality, such as `super_editor`.

### Moving Forward
I would encourage you and @justinmc and the Flutter team in general to begin approaching IME communication in a more structured and substantive way. The current pattern seems to be to deliver a little functionality, wait for another issue to be filed, and then start a new investigation into that all over again. It seems to result in decisions that are based on a relatively superficial understanding of the underlying platform IME. I, too, have a relatively superficial understanding of the platform IME, but I'm also not solely responsible for designing Flutter's API for that communication. 

Text input, and even general content input, is absolutely fundamental to application user interfaces. It happens everywhere, and it happens with great diversity. These IME interactions, which we've discussed across a growing number of tickets, are absolute table stakes in the long run for Flutter. 

The Flutter team should understand the platform IME as deeply as the Flutter team understands how to display the Flutter view on the platform, how to integrate with the touch event system on the platform, how to connect to the v-sync signal on the platform, etc. There should be at least one developer on the team, if not multiple developers, who have expert level knowledge of platform IME APIs and behaviors. Because, again, that behavior is absolutely fundamental to Flutter. It's fundamental to all user interfaces.

I think that taking IME issues seriously means establishing foundational assets to help educate the team, and keep the team up to date. For example, you might:

- Write up Wiki or website docs for how you believe the IME APIs work on the various platforms. Include code snippets that demonstrate your understanding. These documents can be circulated around the team, and publicly. Other developers can correct you if they see that you've described something that isn't quite right. You can also circulate those internally with the Android team to at least get absolute experts on that side of things. But I bet you could even find some current or former iOS team devs to help, too.
- Run a diverse set of input through each platform's IME, e.g., text input that's completely correct, text input that uses spell check, text input with emojis, text input with key swiping, text input with links, text input with images, text input on GBoard vs Samsung, text input with dictation. Take the IME events from each of those real-world examples and put those IME events in Flutter IME test suites so that the world can see what real events look like, and they can see Flutter handling those events. These tests wouldn't assert much of anything - they would just run those real-world event streams through a typical Flutter widget tree and make sure that nothing goes crazy.
- Add integration tests for whichever of these keyboard tests you can manage. My guess is that dictation isn't possible, but maybe there's a test API for that, too. I'm sure there would be fewer integration tests than there would be example tests from the point above, but the purpose of the integration tests would be to ensure, roughly, that nothing major has changed in real world IME behavior as compared to when you collected the events from the point above.

I'm sure I could come up with many more suggestions, too. But the overarching point is that IME isn't going anywhere. This communication will only become strictly more complex, and more important as time moves forward. Therefore, I would strongly encourage you two, and the team, to start building a strong foundation for IME delta and content communication, rather than occasionally react to bug reports and/or undocumented behavior reports.

This ticket seems to indicate that there is a fundamental IME behavior, which, at best, is not understood by the Flutter team. I think this is a great opportunity to begin instituting the suggestions above, and perhaps others, too. This is a good opportunity to start publishing what you *think* is happening in the IME, show the evidence you have to believe that, and then start iterating on that understanding until you've worked through the unknowns. Only when the unknowns are resolved, does it make sense to start engineering solutions on top.

> Does iOS notify the client when dictation starts and stops? If so, is Flutter forwarding this information? If not, Flutter certainly should. Because if this problem is unique to dictation, then we may be able to create an alternate code path to deal with this specifically.

After some more investigation I think this potential solution is not feasible given the API's publicly available to us. `UITextInputMode` does not provide enough information for us to determine that dictation has started. https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uitextinput/1614507-dictationrecordingdidend Does provide a way to detect that dictation has ended, but without knowing when it started this won't help this specific issue. I've tried plugging into other dictation related API's but they're all called after `dictationRecordingDidEnd` so they were not useful.

Similarly on Android I could not find an API that would notify us on the start/end of dictation.

I understand your position that Flutter's transformation of events is currently unproven while Android and IOS IME API's are proven. This will require more investigation on my part, given everything you said I suspect that maybe if Flutter did not transform those events for example gave you a straight replacement for all dictations instead of transformed insertions/deletions then a `/n` would never be detected and therefore the scenario where `super_editor` inserts a hidden period at a new line (It would be helpful if you could provide the code snippet where this happens)  would not happen for dictation and this would not be an issue. Though that is a very specific case, and it may not solve other issues.

> Moving Forward

I see your points and definitely agree that Text input is fundamental to lots of applications. I think I will need to re-visit my overview of how platform IME's work to figure out a way forward considering the growing number of issues regarding TextEditingDeltas. Some issues seem to be interrelated and some also seem to be a result of the editing states on the framework and engine side not being completely in-sync. Thank you for the suggestions, I will keep them in mind when moving forward with this issue and others.
@Renzo-Olivares would you like to collect specific IME interaction use-cases from us? Would you like to do some of this together so that we can encourage some forward progress?
@matthew-carroll It would be great to collect those IME use-cases and collaborate on this. I'll reach out on discord next week so we can sync up regarding this.

This issue is assigned to @Renzo-Olivares but has had no recent status updates. Please consider unassigning this issue if it is not going to be addressed in the near future. This allows people to have a clearer picture of what work is actually planned. Thanks!
@Renzo-Olivares Any updates on this? We're getting lots of users complaining about their content being deleted immediately after dictating on iOS. It's a very bad experience that I am unable to fix.

Hi @Jethro87, I've been working on the general issue of detecting dictation on iOS but haven't made much headway. I have been able to detect dictation on versions lower than iOS 16, but ideally a solution would work on all versions.

Another solution i'm exploring is to stop synthesizing some of the replacement deltas into insertion deltas on iOS. This processing of the raw deltas was done mostly because of some Android specific behavior described below and it could potentially be loosened for iOS.
> iirc the decision for this was because on Android it was observed that the composing region was always active resulting in constant replacements instead of an insertion or deletion. I think some context for this decision can be found here https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/28175#discussion_r698937876 . I don't believe that this abstraction of information is causing any issue with regards to this problem, is there cases where you think this might cause an issue?

Recently, I've also been having trouble reproducing this issue on `super_editor`s latest branch. Are there any concrete steps for reproducing this? What devices / iOS versions is the behavior being observed on? Reproducing IME bugs is very frustrating for everyone, almost all the time. It doesn't necessarily have to be this way. Flutter could introduce tooling, such as IME interaction within integration tests, which would remove a lot of the burden and variability. Instead of treating that as a temporary frustration within this particular ticket, I would encourage you and @justinmc to think of it as a pervasive problem within IME based development, and look at it as an opportunity to solve your frustrations, and everyone else's frustrations, at the same time.

This looks like a serious bug. As a workaround, you could try using a native `<textarea>` or `<input>` HTML tag and add it to the app using `HtmlElementView` ([this package](https://pub.dev/packages/flutter_native_text_input) uses a similar approach on iOS).

I also notice that there are two overlapped bubbles from Chrome Android demo at https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/95958#issuecomment-1011864727.

This is still definitely a P1.  I see a few different issues:
- The selection overlay on iOS is occurring because there's no way to hide the underlying input's selection highlighting on iOS (see https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/99918)
- Multiline selection/cursor issues.  We don't currently sync scroll state and I see this causing a few issues re: multiline because the context menu always interacts with the underlying element.  I think this is especially prevalent on mobile because the context menu is a much more frequent part of the UX. 

Tagging this issue here (https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/120613) as this aims to solve the above. 

In the intermediary, we could try to sync scroll state to improve the multiline experience and eliminate some of the issues we're seeing.

issues.title: EditText box in TLHC handles resize and offset changes poorly

### Is there an existing issue for this?

- [X] I have searched the [existing issues](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues)
- [X] I have read the [guide to filing a bug](https://flutter.dev/docs/resources/bug-reports)

### Steps to reproduce

Filing this issue coz comments are disabled on the original one: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/112712
We thought we found a way to use TLHC as a substitute for HC when wrapping a native textfield.
The solution suggested by @johnmccutchan (see here https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/112712#issuecomment-1807704679)
indeed resolves the issue related to Magnifier lense, but a new issue originated as a consequence of that fix.

Basically, due to that `override`, the surface gets rendered with some delay and the platform view bumps when its position is changed (in our case the inputted text bumps when a new line is added).

Please do not discontinue HC as long as a fix for this is found. We've no alternative to it and we've also invested serious money/time on this.

### Expected results

We would love to use TLHC but texture-rendering must be as smooth as with HC in the above described scenario.

### Actual results

Platform View bumps when its position is changed on the scene.

### Code sample

Code available here:
https://github.com/delfme/flutter-native-text-input-repo

The plugin uses TLHC and issue is reproducible
https://github.com/collinjackson/flutter-native-text-input/blob/1ef42d30462d97a3fafc856e356af83e106fe6b6/lib/flutter_native_text_input.dart#L334

Fork the plugin and change TLHC to HC to see that no issue occurs with HC.

The platform view embeds an EditText widget which draws the border around the text, the text, and the cursor.

When in TLHC mode we render the EditText widget into a Canvas just as normal Android does, on the same Android UI thread. The only difference is that we save the rendered canvas in a texture and draw the texture on the screen.

In the frame where the text disappears you can see the border, no text, and the text cursor positioned as if there is no text. This suggests that the EditText widget is drawing itself correctly but the text contents is cleared.

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/assets/53510751/ea7ab85f-f114-415b-a2a9-b0ef4cb13015

I spent some time looking at things this week and I have to say I'm not really sure how this would work. The gist is about what you would think: there is no synchronization of rendering when resizing the texture layer, so your choices are to either partially drop frames (current) or to rendering the old contents either stretched or positioned incorrectly (without alignment it won't be possible to determine where to place the smaller rectangle in all circumstances).

Lets go through the basic sequence of events that lead to the scenario above:

1. [UI Thread] A message is sent to the platform thread with new layout information. 
2. [Platform Thread] A message is receieved that the platform view should be resized.
      a. The current image reader is discarded. A null image is added to the texture entry immediately.
      b. A new image reader is created. A listener is attached that will update the texture entry with a new image.
      c. The layout properties of the underlying view are updated. This should eventually trigger a new frame.
3. [Raster thread] A message is recieved that there is a new texture entry, a forced frame is scheduled if 1) didn't already schedule a frame.
     a. The null entry is received. No work is done, but since the image reader has been disposed/closed, the image will be empty.
4. [Raster thread] A message is receieved that there is a new texture entry. A forced frame is scheduled and the updated contents are rendered.

We're dealing with all three threads here, so 2 and 3/4 are more or less happening at the same time. For this specific example, generally the problem seems to be that 1) will trigger a frame and that frame might be rasterized after/during the old texture entry is torn down but before the new texture entry is populated.

Actually, in general, the only reason this seems to work at all is that in most cases we don't care if the Dart Ui and the Android UI are perfectly in sync. But resizing breaks this - in the same way that window resizing breaks things on desktop.
To fix this, when resizing you'd need a way to ensure that the frame scheduled by the actions in 1) aren't triggered until 3) is completed.

Another way to fix it would be to change the API such that it was not possible for the Flutter UI to treat the platform view as having been resizing until after the new frame is produced, then update the image reader disposal logic to hold onto the last image until the new image is rendered.

@jonahwilliams might your description above explain also why textfield and native keyboard animations are out of sync, producing a stuttering/jerking effect when keyboard moves up? This is a known issue that nobody was able to fix so far and impairs the UI layout where texfield is placed at the bottom of the screen (ie. chatscreen). 

We are workarounding this by:
1) resizeToAvoidBottomInset : false 
2) wrap textfield inside a Container and assign a bottom margin that will be used to move up textfield when keyboard is opened
3) listen when keyboard is open and grab keyboard height via native
4) update bottom margin and move up the texfield instantly, with margin value == keyboard height

This avoid the conflicts between dart and native (coz textfield is moved up in a snap), but visual result is “meh” - a bump instead of the smooth animation which occurs with native apps.
Thanks @jonahwilliams for the analysis. I will start to experiment with changes.

With https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/49201 applied we no longer drop frames. There is still image stretching though (that is a separate issue we need to resolve).

[chatlayoutdemo2.webm](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/assets/224266/dc5ff2a4-bfd8-4f21-a1d2-161c87ec1702)
@johnmccutchan that’s great, we would like to dismiss HC for textfield. Is the image stretch caused by the size-change? Also, do you see a loading-time improvement with TLHC, coz I dont.

Image stretch is caused by the size-change but we should be able to fix that as well (but in a separate PR).

Haven't looked at loading-time improvements with TLHC yet.

This issue is assigned to @johnmccutchan but has had no recent status updates. Please consider unassigning this issue if it is not going to be addressed in the near future. This allows people to have a clearer picture of what work is actually planned. Thanks!

We know that the stretch (which creates the text bumping effect when tapping Return key) is caused by the platform view's size change. Was wondering if the suggestion from @jonahwilliams about keeping the size until next frame is produced might be a fix.
This issue was assigned to @johnmccutchan but has had no status updates in a long time. To remove any ambiguity about whether the issue is being worked on, the assignee was removed.

Hello, just informing that we upgraded to latest master and this issue still occurs.
Here is the situation:
- if we use HC, we get optimal fidelity and no text bumping issue when user presses the keyboard's Return button. But widget is bit heavy on flutter. We use it everywhere but, for example, we cannot use it if it is in the same scene with a video player, coz video playback would be laggy.

- if we use AndroidView, we have this issue of text bumping anytime user taps the keyboard's Return button. So, as of now, we cannot use TLHC. As mentioned by @johnmccutchan this issue might be due to Image stretch which is caused by the size-change.

Hello,
We now are experiencing poor loading time with the platformView when using the HC (Hybrid Composition). This issue did not exist in the past, leading us to believe that it may be related to recent changes in flutter. The poor loading time is impairing the screen animation, where we see the screen tilting and janks during the animation.

This is a significant problem for us because we cannot use our native EditText box with TLHC due to the resize/offset issue, and we also cannot utilize it with HC effectively.

Additionally, we tried to display a placeholder until the HC is fully loaded but then HC causes the entire screen to repaint with a flickering issue when platformView visibility is set to true. Since the widget is wrapped inside a Consumer, we expected only the widget itself to repaint, rather than the whole screen, but HC is causing a whole screen repaint. 

- Could you please provide any guidance or suggestions on how to resolve these issues? Any recommended fixes or workarounds would be greatly appreciated.
- Can you test screen animation when a platformView with HC is inside the destination screen?

Hello everyone,
We've attached two videos demonstrating the regression we're facing with Hybrid Composition (HC):

### HC (Hybrid Composition):

The widget itself functions perfectly (including the loupe effect), but the screen animation is noticeably janky.
NOTE: We cannot address this issue on the Dart side by using a placeholder and delaying the creation of the platformView until the screen animation is complete, because changing the visibility of the platformView causes Flutter to repaint the entire screen with a flickering effect (even if screen is empty).

### TLHC (Texture Layer Hybrid Composition):

The screen animation is smooth, but the widget fails to display the loupe and is affected by the resize and offset issue.
NOTE: Overriding the `getRenderMode()` method in TLHC allows the loupe to appear, but the resize and offset problem persists. Additionally, we observed that this override leads to several UI and performance-related issues.

```
override fun getRenderMode(): RenderMode {
    return RenderMode.texture
}
```

We would appreciate any guidance on how to address the issue with HC.

We can confirm that HC has always worked perfectly for us and has never affected screen animations. Maybe a recent change to flutter engine caused the janky screen animation with HC, can you please have a look? 

### VIDEO HC
Please notice the janks while opening the screen.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a3a074f8-c739-49de-8893-caca86af8419


### VIDEO TLHC
No janks while opening the screen, but no loupe + the resize and offset issue.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b1a1b01c-3586-4f16-98b5-cef8608edfb0

Here is a video demonstrating the flickering issue caused by Hybrid Composition (HC). As previously mentioned, delaying the loading of the platformView allows us to achieve a smooth screen animation. However, the moment HC is rendered, the flickering effect appears.

### VIDEO of flickering caused HC:
https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/cea55b17-1566-4e75-bdcf-d28e5666ac40

It occurred to us that the flickering might also be the root cause of the janky screen animation. When we don't delay the loading of the platformView, the flickering happens during the screen animation itself, which could be contributing to the overall laggy experience.

## Steps to Reproduce

<!-- You must include full steps to reproduce so that we can reproduce the problem. -->

1. Run `flutter create bug`.
2. Update the files as follows:
`lib/main.dart` should become:
<details> 
<summary>minimal code sample</summary> 

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';
import 'package:flutter/services.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  // This widget is the root of your application.
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      title: 'Double Focus Jump Demo',
      home: Scaffold(
        appBar: AppBar(
          title: Text('Double Focus Jump Demo'),
        ),
        body: Column(
          children: <Widget>[
            MyTextField(),
            MyTextField(),
            MyTextField(),
          ],
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}

class MyTextField extends StatelessWidget {
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Focus(
      skipTraversal: true,
      onKey: (focus, keyEvent) {
        if (keyEvent.logicalKey == LogicalKeyboardKey.enter) {
          focus.nextFocus();
          return KeyEventResult.handled;
        }
        return KeyEventResult.ignored;
      },
      child: TextFormField(),
    );
  }
}
``` 

</details>

3. Focus one of the text fields, then hit enter. <!-- describe how to reproduce the problem -->

**Expected results:** The focus will pass to the next text field. <!-- what did you want to see? -->

**Actual results:** It passes to the next text field for a split second and then skips again to the next one. It always does exactly that: move to the next text field for a split second, then move to the one after. <!-- what did you see? -->

issues.title: TextInputClient/DeltaTextInputClient has different behaviors on MacOS and Windows
issues.body:
## Steps to Reproduce On macos, `updateEditingValueWithDeltas` was invoked after `TextInputConnection.setEditingState`. On windows, no `updateEditingValueWithDeltas` invoked after `TextInputConnection.setEditingState`. 1. Execute `flutter run` on the code sample 2. Click text to call `TextInputConnection.setEditingState`. 3. Watch console print out. ![捕获](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/15059084/178477836-f50bded9-34ed-42db-ab33-6f079a6c721e.PNG)
Code sample ```dart import 'package:flutter/material.dart'; import 'package:flutter/services.dart'; void main() { runApp(const MyApp()); } class MyApp extends StatelessWidget { const MyApp({Key? key}) : super(key: key); // This widget is the root of your application. @override Widget build(BuildContext context) { return MaterialApp( title: 'Flutter Demo', theme: ThemeData( // This is the theme of your application. // // Try running your application with "flutter run". You'll see the // application has a blue toolbar. Then, without quitting the app, try // changing the primarySwatch below to Colors.green and then invoke // "hot reload" (press "r" in the console where you ran "flutter run", // or simply save your changes to "hot reload" in a Flutter IDE). // Notice that the counter didn't reset back to zero; the application // is not restarted. primarySwatch: Colors.blue, ), home: const MyHomePage(title: 'Flutter Demo Home Page'), ); } } class MyHomePage extends StatefulWidget { const MyHomePage({Key? key, required this.title}) : super(key: key); // This widget is the home page of your application. It is stateful, meaning // that it has a State object (defined below) that contains fields that affect // how it looks. // This class is the configuration for the state. It holds the values (in this // case the title) provided by the parent (in this case the App widget) and // used by the build method of the State. Fields in a Widget subclass are // always marked "final". final String title; @override State createState() => _MyHomePageState(); } class _MyHomePageState extends State { late final TextInputConnection _connection; @override void initState() { _connection = TextInput.attach(_InputClient(), const TextInputConfiguration( enableDeltaModel: true, )); _connection.setEditingState(TextEditingValue.empty); _connection.show(); super.initState(); } @override Widget build(BuildContext context) { // This method is rerun every time setState is called, for instance as done // by the _incrementCounter method above. // // The Flutter framework has been optimized to make rerunning build methods // fast, so that you can just rebuild anything that needs updating rather // than having to individually change instances of widgets. return Scaffold( appBar: AppBar( // Here we take the value from the MyHomePage object that was created by // the App.build method, and use it to set our appbar title. title: Text(widget.title), ), body: Align( alignment: Alignment.center, child: GestureDetector( onTap: () { final String text = DateTime.now().toString(); print('setEditingState $text'); _connection.setEditingState(TextEditingValue( text: text )); }, child: const Text('Click'), ), ), ); } } class _InputClient implements DeltaTextInputClient { @override void connectionClosed() { } @override AutofillScope? get currentAutofillScope => throw UnimplementedError(); @override TextEditingValue? get currentTextEditingValue => throw UnimplementedError(); @override void insertTextPlaceholder(Size size) { } @override void performAction(TextInputAction action) { } @override void performPrivateCommand(String action, Map data) { } @override void removeTextPlaceholder() { } @override void showAutocorrectionPromptRect(int start, int end) { } @override void showToolbar() { } @override void updateEditingValue(TextEditingValue value) { } @override void updateEditingValueWithDeltas(List textEditingDeltas) { print('updateEditingState ${textEditingDeltas.map((e) => e.runtimeType).join(',')}'); } @override void updateFloatingCursor(RawFloatingCursorPoint point) { } } ```

issues.title: [desktop] Chinese characters cannot be input when TextField is surrounded by ToolTip
issues.body:
During development, we found that when "TextField" was wrapped by "ToolTip" chinese input could not be performed normally.

The code is simple：
```
Column(
          mainAxisAlignment: MainAxisAlignment.center,
          children: <Widget>[
            Tooltip(child: TextField(),message: "1",),
            Tooltip(child: TextField(),message: "2",),
            Tooltip(child: TextField(),message: "3",),
            Tooltip(child: TextField(),message: "4",),
          ],
        )
```

The result is：

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4360198/212610715-7e82a2cd-f8b9-4472-b69f-4eee9ff27b1d.mp4

After testing, it was found that，there are problems in version 3.3.10 and version 3.3.9, but there are no problems in version 3.3.8

Since my duplicate issue has been closed, and this one has been open for several years, I've been looking for a workaround that allows me to use this feature while waiting for the problem to be solved at platform level.

I have published a package at https://pub.dev/packages/dead_keys with an ad-hoc text editing controller that should handle these cases. It's a library still at alpha level, I'd just like to know what you think and if it's a viable way.

The package by @aleripe is very good however is not perfect (eg doesnt handle correctly TextFormField with obscureText: true as used for password fields). I am voting for implementing support for these accented keys into Flutter.

issues.title: Textfield obscureText interaction exception
issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce

1. First click on a textfield whose obscureText is false(true)
2. Click again on a textfield whose obscureText is true (false)

### Expected results

The keyboard should not disappear and reappear. It should directly determine the keyboard type and focus on the new textfield.

### Actual results

The keyboard animation pops up and disappears abnormally. The cursor disappears and needs to be clicked again to focus.
 
Upgrading from 3.10.0 to 3.16.0 appears, in HarmonyOS 4,

### Code sample

<details open><summary>Code sample</summary>

class MyHomePage extends StatefulWidget {

}

issues.title: Autocorrect tooltips don't appear on iOS
issues.body:
## Steps to Reproduce

When using a native iOS app without text prediction enabled, there is a pop-up that indicates when autocorrections are going to occur, and when backspacing on a correction a pop-up appears that gives alternative choices for the word.

In flutter, the autocorrections happen silently and require backspacing into the word to make the correction.  See the gif below for a comparison of the behavior.

Flutter should either integrate with the iOS autocorrection system to provide the popups or emulate them.  This is a pretty big usability concern which makes it more difficult to enter text because the corrections are harder to notice and harder to fix.

![text_correction](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/103489/32534122-0182048a-c409-11e7-8879-606857aff89e.gif)

## Logs

N/A, this is a design issue.

I spoke with @GaryQian and @HansMuller about this.

Proposed solutions so far generally fall into these 2 categories:

- Blocking execution

Delegate the [`func firstRect(for range: UITextRange) -> CGRect`](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uitextinput/1614570-firstrect) computation to the framework using platform channels, block the platform thread until the result comes back. See https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/4358 for potential problems with blocking the platform thread.

- Asynchronous prefetching 

Keep a copy of `Paragraph` data (or whatever information it takes) in the iOS text input plugin, and keep it up to date with the framework using platform channels. Implement [`func firstRect(for range: UITextRange) -> CGRect`](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uitextinput/1614570-firstrect) using the engine copy of information. 
  
This introduces a race condition: when text is entered via the software keyboard, the iOS text input plugin sends the update back to the framework (which in turn triggers an update in `Paragraph` data that needs to be synced back to the engine side), and at the same time the keyboard calls [`func firstRect(for range: UITextRange) -> CGRect`](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uitextinput/1614570-firstrect). There's no guarantee the updated `Paragraph` data will arrive in the engine before [`func firstRect(for range: UITextRange) -> CGRect`](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uitextinput/1614570-firstrect) returns. And there's no way to tell iOS to update the rect should the `Paragraph` data arrive late.

@LongCatIsLooong I don't know if your comments was meant to indicate that this issue was blocked or reconsidered, but I wanted to add some weight to this.

We're building a [secure messaging app](https://www.braver.net/en/) for healthcare, and 50% of the user experience is around typing. Currently on iOS, unless you set your iPhone to have the 3 autosuggestions above the keyboard, the typing experience is simply broken. Some replacements that are usually shown using the white tooltip before being applied do not appear, creating confusion and straight inability to prevent said replacement.

Below, on the left is a reference working example, using Notion (probably react-native or something like this), and on the right, our app using Flutter.

![2020-07-14 06 44 40](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1283/87417143-c0003d80-c59d-11ea-8768-05770cc3b06f.gif)

I want to emphasize this: the experience is _currently broken_. I cannot tell our users to go in the settings and change their keyboard specifically for our app. It _is_ high priority, not a minor visual glitch.

What's especially alarming to me is the lack of priority on the team side and the lack of severity on the community side. We have started recommending React Native for any heavily text-editing based apps that are going on the iOS App Store because of this broken experience.

That's a very difficult position to be in. Maybe someone from the flutter team can give us a high level strategy for implementing it. I don't have the bandwidth to work on this feature ATM but if they already have an idea of how to implement then someone may have the time to achieve it.

I wish there was a way to turn on the autocorrect section on the keyboard when in a Flutter app. That would be a totally acceptable shim for the time being IMHO. Maybe there is a way? I have no idea.

@matehat the gif on the right confuses me a bit, at least the background highlight should have shown up when the text was autocorrected. (I suppose you're using the latest stable or something newer) Could you share your iOS keyboard configuration used in that clip?

For autocorrect as previously mentioned in `func firstRect(for range: UITextRange) -> CGRect` a correct `CGRect` needs to be returned by that method for iOS to correctly position the text background highlight and the tooltip. Currently we don't have an easy way to get the correct `CGRect` synchronously, so we have 2 options:

1. Return `CGRectZero` to prevent iOS from drawing the highlight and the tooltip, and send the `UITextRange` provided in the call to the framework to let Flutter draw the highlight. This is the current approach, it should work well for the highlight but we lose the tooltip.

2. Try our best to predict the correct `Rect` and let iOS draw the highlight & the tooltip. I made a POC that seems to work well enough for #9344, but autocorrect is a bit different:
    * For positioning the IME tooltip(#9344), we just need the bottom left point (or the bottom right point). For autocorrect the entire `Rect` needs to be on point, so the background highlight iOS shows indicates the correct range of the text that will be autocorrected. 
    *  For positioning the IME tooltip(#9344), iOS marks the text before calling `func firstRect(for range: UITextRange) -> CGRect`, so we have some idea about what the `range` is going to be before `func firstRect(for range: UITextRange) -> CGRect` gets called. iOS doesn't mark the text that needs to be autocorrected before calling `func firstRect(for range: UITextRange) -> CGRect`, so we have to entirely rely on the `range` parameter, which we don't have access to before `func firstRect(for range: UITextRange) -> CGRect` (at which point it's already too late to request the rect information from the framework). Using the current word works in some cases, but as shown in @matehat's gif clip on the left, iOS may autocorrect multiple words.

It seems the same `Rect` returned is used for both the highlight and the tooltip, and returning a `Rect` that has 0 width (or height) would also prevent both the tooltip and the highlight from showing up, so we can't just tell iOS to hide the highlight and show the tooltip. 

I guess turning off `autocorrect` in `TextField` could be a workaround for the time being, if you would rather not have autocorrect on the text field if there's no tooltip.

Yeah, this issue is about the autocorrect suggestions that appear next to the iOS cursor while you're typing regardless of the styling of the app. Currently it works as expected if you have `Predictive` setting turned on in `General => Keyboards`. However, if that's turned off these tooltips do not appear when typing. *Taken from iPhone 11 Simulator, iOS 13.6* There are two solutions that I can think of. 1) Implement the autocorrect tooltips for when `Predictive` is off. or 2) Find a way to force `Predictive` setting whenever in a Flutter app.

It doesn't seem like option 2 is possible if I look at all the documentation and questions around predictive UI.

I think showing the tooltip is more important than showing the exact highlighted region, since the tooltip gives you the ability to accept or refuse a correction. 

Is there a way to show the tooltip and position it approximately right?

Sorry I just saw this part of @LongCatIsLooong comment:

> It seems the same Rect returned is used for both the highlight and the tooltip, and returning a Rect that has 0 width (or height) would also prevent both the tooltip and the highlight from showing up, so we can't just tell iOS to hide the highlight and show the tooltip.

Maybe a minimal but non-zero width, and a height deducted from the text's known line height would do?

I found an approach that seems to work well for https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/9344, save for a few edge cases. I'll see if that works for the autocorrect tooltip too. The highlighted region must accurately indicate the range of the autocorrect otherwise it can be misleading. Maybe we can override `addSubview` to prevent the highlight from being added.

@LongCatIsLooong, there is already a precedent for blocking call from engine to UI thread in `FlutterKeyboardManager.mm`. It runs nested CFRunLoop in `kMessageLoopCFRunLoopMode` until it gets the response.

Looking at the code I am wondering a bit what happens if the UI isolate gets terminated when within the inner loop. This is a bit underdocumented and I'm not 100% sure it is handled correctly.

Arguably this is playing with fire a little bit, but in this case it should be perfectly safe since the UI thread action is completely synchronous and UI thread can not do blocking calls to other threads.

We do similar thing in `super_drag_and_drop` to get items for `UIDragInteractionDelegate` and `UIContextMenuInteractionDelegate` (though using FFI instead platform channel in order to make sure not to deadlock when isolate is terminated before platform code receives the response). But in our case it gets bit more complicated because the call also involves getting image data and thus waits for raster thread, which under some circumstances is blocked on platform thread. Still, it works quite well even though because of raster thread involvement we need to pump the event loop in default mode as well.

But the situation here is simpler and the call to `TextInputPlugin` can be synchronous, so the approach from `FlutterKeyboardManager.mm` should work just fine.

This issue as a whole seems to have regressed with the changes introduced in iOS 17 Beta. Namely, the autocorrect “highlight rectangle” is now always highlighting the _most recently entered character_, regardless of whether there is an autocorrect pending or not.
